title: We Are the Last People Who Know How It Works
titleZh: 我们是最后知道它是如何运作的人
author: cylo
relevanceReason: 命中 1 个 AI/科技关键词。
summaryRaw: You only ever know the things you can lose to.
FILED 2026-06-26 WORDS 773 READ 4 MIN BY CYRUS LOPEZ

To play a computer game in in the 1990s, you first had to understand how the computer worked. So you learned. You opened files like autoexec.bat and you read them. Sometimes you built a boot disk for a single game. A floppy whose entire reason to exist was to start the computer in the configuration that one program demanded. A ten year old did this. I did this. You did it because you wanted to play badly enough, and the machine would not let you play until you had learned a little of how it worked. That was the arrangement. The machine had terms! Everything had terms then. The modem sang its negotiation out loud, and that shriek was two machines arguing about how to speak to each other, and after enough times you could hear when the argument was going south and the call was about to drop. You set little jumpers on drives with your fingernail. You knew which interrupt your sound card answered to, because if you guessed wrong it answered to nothing. The machine was made of edges and you cut yourself on them, and that is how you found out where they were. This might sound like I’m complaining, but I’m not. The difficulty was the knowledge. You came to know that machine the way you come to know anything that pushes back. The resistance was the whole medium. You only ever know the things that you can lose to. Which is what makes the machines and services we are building now so strange, and so easy to mistake for an old story about convenience. We describe it as the last convenience, the AI assistant, the thing that finally sands off the friction. And it does. You say what you want and it appears. It never makes you read a config file. It never sets terms. It rearranges itself around your sentence, says sorry when you frown, and tries again. It is the most accommodating thing anyone has ever built. And a machine that cannot challenge you is a thing you cannot know. You can only use it. Some of us keep telling the story of this moment as a loss of competence. The graybeards are aging out, nobody compiles their kernel anymore, and someday something deep will break and there will be no one left who can climb down and fix it. Maybe. But I think competence is the part that’s fine. The knowledge is not in danger, in fact, it has never been safer. The AI models have read every manual that no human reads. They will recite, flawlessly and forever, exactly how all machines work. If this were only about competence, it would be the most secure moment in the history of computing. What is dying is acquaintance. The plain, unglamorous intimacy of having fought a particular machine, and lost, and gone back, and finally felt the thing give. We are about to lean on these machines harder than we have ever leaned on any tool, and we are going to know them less well than I knew a beige computer in 1995 that wouldn’t run a game until I had rearranged its bits by hand. More dependent than ever. Less acquainted than ever. Both at once, and for the same reason. The kids coming up will not feel this as a loss, and they are not wrong not to. You cannot miss a relationship you never had. They will have a tool that does everything and asks for nothing, and they will be as easy with it as you are with the light switch you never once thought about. There is nothing sad in their ease. The grief is entirely ours. We happen to be the last people who knew a machine the only way a machine was ever really known: by having it be difficult. And we are also the last people who will know that anything is gone. I went looking the other night for a recording of a modem connecting. People keep them around like pressed flowers. I played one back for no reason except to hear it. The dial tones, the hiss, the rush of static, and then the sudden hush when the two machines finally agreed on something. My modern computer played it instantly and perfectly. Without effort, the way it does everything now. I know that sound by heart. People will never know the machine that just played the sound back to me. Not the way I knew the one that used to make it. It doesn’t need me to. That’s what we built. That’s what we wanted.
summaryZh: You only ever know the things you can lose to.
FILED 2026-06-26 WORDS 773 READ 4 MIN BY CYRUS LOPEZ

comments.author: CommieBobDole
comments.textRawHtml:
The issue with this is that we don't know how it works. Generally speaking, we know how the level of abstraction that we were born with works. We might have some understanding of one or two previous levels, but that decreases the farther down you go. We might understand the next level, and some of the next after that, but eventually people will be making things that we don't have the context to understand without having to unlearn a lot of what we know now.

I'm old enough to see this process in action; I used to be young and in possession of esoteric knowledge that made me infinitely in demand and now most of the things that young people have esoteric knowledge about is things that I don't particularly care about, and I'm left with a lot of finely honed skills to solve problems that have mostly been abstracted away.
comments.textZhHtml:
问题是我们不知道它是如何工作的。一般来说，我们知道我们与生俱来的抽象水平是如何运作的。我们可能对之前的一两个关卡有一定的了解，但是越往下了解就越少。我们可能会理解下一个层次，以及之后的一些层次，但最终人们将创造出我们没有背景来理解的东西，而不必忘记我们现在所知道的很多东西。

我已经足够大了，可以亲眼目睹这个过程的实施；以前我年轻，深奥的知识让我无限需求，现在年轻人深奥的知识大多是我不太关心的东西，而我留下了很多磨练出来的技能来解决大部分被抽象掉的问题。

children.author: staindk
children.textRawHtml: I do think there's something to be said for the big difference in the current abstraction layer jump - the loss of determinism. It is meaningfully different.
children.textZhHtml: 我确实认为当前抽象层跳跃的巨大差异是有道理的——决定论的丧失。这是有意义的不同。

children.author: JumpCrisscross
children.textRawHtml: Plenty of physical (and biological) processes are subjectively non-deterministic outside clean rooms. Doesn’t mean our ancestors couldn’t forge and selectively breed.
children.textZhHtml: 在洁净室外，许多物理（和生物）过程在主观上是不确定的。并不意味着我们的祖先无法锻造和选择性繁殖。

children.author: mrob
children.textRawHtml: There's a big difference between "deterministic + noise" non-determinism and "intelligent agent" non-determinism. Only the former can be statistically modeled to characterize and work around the noise with any reliability. If you measure hallucination rate for one prompt it tells you nothing about hallucination rate for another prompt.
children.textZhHtml: “确定性+噪声”非确定性和“智能代理”非确定性之间存在很大差异。只有前者可以进行统计建模，以可靠地表征和解决噪声问题。如果您测量一个提示的幻觉率，它不会告诉您另一提示的幻觉率。

children.author: JumpCrisscross
children.textRawHtml:
> There's a big difference between "deterministic + noise" non-determinism and "intelligent agent" non-determinism

I think I agree, but could you expand?

My point is humans have made progress on understanding systems without clean determinism. Genetics and cosmology is technically deterministic, practically speaking, but I don’t think that’s accessible to pre-modern societies. Instead, trends and tendencies were observed, acted and capitalized on.
children.textZhHtml:
> “确定性+噪声”非确定性和“智能体”非确定性有很大区别

我想我同意，但是你能扩展一下吗？

我的观点是，人类在理解没有明确决定论的系统方面取得了进展。实际上，遗传学和宇宙学在技术上是确定性的，但我认为前现代社会无法做到这一点。相反，人们观察趋势和倾向，采取行动并加以利用。

children.author: mrob
children.textRawHtml:
One big difference is the former doesn't "know" it's being experimented on. Gregor Mendel was able to grow large numbers of pea plants and identify patterns in their inheritance because the non-determinism followed consistent statistical laws. A plant can't suddenly decide to follow different laws of genetics because it saw a biologist.

On the other hand, an LLM "knows" what a benchmark is, and is capable of detecting benchmark-like scenarios. A prompt that appears reliable in testing may fail unexpected in the real world. In this way, an LLM is like a stock market (also composed of intelligent agents): the act of experimenting on it changes its properties. Such systems are never reliably predictable.
children.textZhHtml:
一个很大的区别是前者不“知道”它正在被试验。格雷戈尔·孟德尔能够种植大量豌豆并识别其遗传模式，因为非决定论遵循一致的统计定律。植物不可能因为看到了生物学家而突然决定遵循不同的遗传学定律。

另一方面，法学硕士“知道”基准是什么，并且能够检测类似基准的场景。在测试中看似可靠的提示在现实世界中可能会意外失败。这样，法学硕士就像一个股票市场（也由智能代理组成）：对其进行实验的行为会改变其属性。这样的系统永远无法可靠地预测。

children.author: klabb3
children.textRawHtml:
In engineering you have tolerances to deal with non-determinism. ”Within these bounds” and ”given these assumptions then…” is the foundation of building something _on top of_ those things. LLMs are the same, it relies on heavily exact Turing machines as input but its output is entirely unstable. Even if you can get determinism it will never get anything resembling ”bounds” out of the box. That makes it a poor foundation for building on top of. Ie it’s not a screwdriver, it’s the monkey who’s holding it.

I do not understand the need to argue that monkeys are better than screwdrivers at screwing. Just let the monkey be the best version of a monkey.
children.textZhHtml:
在工程中，您需要容忍来处理非确定性。 “在这些范围内”和“给定这些假设……”是在这些东西之上构建东西的基础。 LLM 是相同的，它依赖于高度精确的图灵机作为输入，但其输出完全不稳定。即使你能得到决定论，它也永远不会得到任何类似于“界限”的东西。这使得它的基础较差。也就是说，这不是一把螺丝刀，而是拿着它的猴子。

我不明白有必要争论猴子比螺丝刀更擅长拧螺丝。让猴子成为最好的猴子吧。

children.author: imoverclocked
children.textRawHtml:
You’ve clearly not seen monkeys. /sarcasm

I would argue that management is a better discipline to pull from for employing LLMs; It is better equipped to deal with non-determinism and going completely off the rails.
children.textZhHtml:
你肯定没见过猴子。 /讽刺

我认为，对于雇用法学硕士来说，管理是一门更好的学科。它更有能力应对非确定性和完全脱轨的情况。

children.author: gonzalohm
children.textRawHtml: I think it's safe to say that computing should be mostly deterministic. I know compilers use heuristics that are stochastic, but come on. Imagine if you were given a layer of abstraction that randomly flipped bits from time to time... That's not an abstraction, it's random programming.
children.textZhHtml: 我认为可以肯定地说，计算应该大部分是确定性的。我知道编译器使用随机的启发式方法，但是来吧。想象一下，如果给你一个抽象层，它时不时地随机翻转位......那不是抽象，而是随机编程。

children.author: JumpCrisscross
children.textRawHtml:
> Imagine if you were given a layer of abstraction that randomly flipped bits from time to time

To be fair, this isn’t a bad description of human vision.
children.textZhHtml:
> 想象一下，如果给你一个抽象层，它会不时地随机翻转位

公平地说，这是对人类视觉的一个不错的描述。

children.author: zild3d
children.textRawHtml:
> Imagine if you were given a layer of abstraction that randomly flipped bits from time to time... That's not an abstraction, it's random programming.

Computing has never really been deterministic all the way down. Storage gets corrupted, RAM has soft errors, networks drop/reorder packets, schedulers race, caches go stale, distributed systems partition, query planners change plans, ...

Obviously those become tolerable because we've developed layers of contracts and understanding the bounds around them. Error correcting codes, checksums, retries, consensus, idempotency, false-positive rates, SLAs, etc.

If the abstraction is “delegate this task to a junior engineer, analyst, lawyer, designer, or support rep,” you dont expect deterministic behavior. There's review, constraints, escalation, checklists, tests, and accountability.
children.textZhHtml:
> 想象一下，如果给你一个抽象层，它时不时地随机翻转位......那不是抽象，而是随机编程。

计算从来都不是真正确定性的。存储损坏、RAM 出现软错误、网络丢弃/重新排序数据包、调度程序竞争、缓存过时、分布式系统分区、查询规划器更改计划……

显然，这些变得可以容忍，因为我们已经开发了多层合约并了解它们周围的界限。纠错码、校验和、重试、共识、幂等性、误报率、SLA 等。

如果抽象是“将此任务委托给初级工程师、分析师、律师、设计师或支持代表”，那么您就不会期望确定性行为。有审查​​、限制、升级、清单、测试和问责制。

children.author: ajuc
children.textRawHtml:
There's a pretty cool article about trying to understand electronics and fix a radio the way we try to understand biology. https://mappingignorance.org/2014/07/02/biologists-cant-unde...

So now instead of improving the tools of biology so we can actually understand it deeply - we increase complexity of IT so we have to rely on muddy, side-effecty tools of biology to try to infer some of the properties of the systems we made. That's depressing.
children.textZhHtml:
有一篇非常酷的文章，讲述了尝试以理解生物学的方式来理解电子学和修理收音机。 https://mappingignorance.org/2014/07/02/biologists-cant-unde...

因此，现在我们不是改进生物学工具，以便我们能够真正深入地理解它，而是增加了 IT 的复杂性，因此我们必须依靠模糊的、有副作用的生物学工具来尝试推断我们所制造的系统的一些属性。真令人沮丧。

children.author: EvanAnderson
children.textRawHtml: I feel crushing sadness when I think about how humans made these wonderful deterministic machines that we can understand and manipulate to do our bidding. Within a scant few generations, though, we've managed to effectively turn them into "magic boxes" that, soon, we'll only be able to poke and prod at when they don't do what we want.
children.textZhHtml: 当我想到人类如何制造这些奇妙的确定性机器，我们可以理解并操纵它们来执行我们的命令时，我感到无比悲伤。然而，在短短几代人的时间里，我们就成功地将它们有效地变成了“魔盒”，很快，我们只能在它们不做我们想要的事情时才能戳和戳。

children.author: pydry
children.textRawHtml:
Our ancestors also had an infant mortality ratio of 4/10.

Determinism is way undervalued.
children.textZhHtml:
我们祖先的婴儿死亡率也为 4/10。

决定论被严重低估了。

children.author: tines
children.textRawHtml: People use the word "determinism" when they really mean something akin to "linearity", i.e. the predictability of a change in input on a change in output. Compilers for example are both deterministic and "more linear" in the sense that I can tell what the output will look like given a change in input (yes yes optimizations violate this to a small degree). LLMs can be made totally deterministic, but a seemingly insignificant change in input can create a drastic change in output, which is the characterstic we don't want.
children.textZhHtml: 当人们真正指的是类似于“线性”的东西时，人们使用“决定论”这个词，即输入变化对输出变化的可预测性。例如，编译器既是确定性的又是“更线性”的，因为我可以告诉输入发生变化时输出会是什么样子（是的，优化在很小程度上违反了这一点）。法学硕士可以完全确定性，但输入中看似微不足道的变化可能会导致输出发生巨大变化，这是我们不想要的特征。

children.author: eikenberry
children.textRawHtml: The generally used term that I'm familiar with for this is "referential transparency", that given the same inputs you'll get the same outputs every time. LLMs can be deterministic (referentially transparent) but almost none are, i.e. when they are given the exact same input they do not return the exact same output.
children.textZhHtml: 我所熟悉的常用术语是“引用透明度”，即给定相同的输入，您每次都会得到相同的输出。 LLM 可以是确定性的（参考透明的），但几乎没有，即当给它们完全相同的输入时，它们不会返回完全相同的输出。

children.author: JumpCrisscross
children.textRawHtml: Wait, what is the light between that definition of referential transparency and determinism?
children.textZhHtml: 等等，参照透明度的定义和决定论之间有什么区别？

children.author: eptcyka
children.textRawHtml: If there is something to be said, I have to ask. What specifically is there to be said?
children.textZhHtml: 如果有什么要说的，我必须问。具体有什么要说的呢？

children.author: zephen
children.textRawHtml:
Non-determinism is not inherently bad. We have had useful non-deterministic tools for (in computer years) several generations.

Non-determinism combined with bugginess?

That's a terrible combination. It is impossible to gradient-descent your way into a working prompt.
children.textZhHtml:
非决定论本质上并不是坏事。 （在计算机时代）几代人以来，我们已经拥有了有用的非确定性工具。

非决定论与错误相结合？

这是一个可怕的组合。 不可能通过梯度下降进入工作提示。

children.author: wseqyrku
children.textRawHtml:
> Non-determinism is not inherently bad.

I would say it is unambiguously defined by the problem you're solving. For example a spellchecker is inherently probabilistic so you shouldn't need hard coded rules here like we did for years. A "bug" then would be considered "weak accuracy" not "crash" or "incorrect behavior", as it might be the case in any other layer.

They should add a term for it, maybe "embarrassingly nondeterministic"
children.textZhHtml:
> 非决定论本质上并不是坏事。

我想说它是由您正在解决的问题明确定义的。例如，拼写检查器本质上是概率性的，因此您不应该像我们多年来那样需要硬编码规则。那么“错误”将被视为“准确性差”，而不是“崩溃”或“不正确的行为”，因为在任何其他层中都可能出现这种情况。

他们应该为此添加一个术语，也许是“令人尴尬的不确定性”

children.author: zephen
children.textRawHtml:
> I would say [whether nondeterminism is bad] is unambiguously defined by the problem you're solving.

Sure. You gave an example where it can work. Another example is something like a PCB or chip layout engine.

That particular domain (layout) is NP-complete. You'll never have an exhaustive brute force search for the optimal layout, but...

You can subsequently easily check whether the produced layout meets all your acceptance criteria or not.

Another example that successfully utilizes non-determinism for good outcomes is the application of genetic algorithms to things like antenna design.

This works because (a) you have a relatively cheap fitness test; and (b) as with real evolution, the mix of combining working designs and randomly introducing mutations often eventually produces outstanding results.

Presumably, if you applied genetic algorithm techniques to, e.g., creating your LLM prompts, you could also get good results, but that would probably quickly get expensive in terms of tokens.

So we're left with people just semi-randomly modifying prompts in order to try to tweak results.

When it works, it can be amazing. When it doesn't work, it's like a brick wall.

I like your "embarrassingly nondeterministic" term, but I somewhat disagree with:

> A "bug" then would be considered "weak accuracy" not "crash" or "incorrect behavior",

When a lawyer asks an LLM for citations of cases that support his position, he is arguably doing something stupid, because embedding an assertion such as "Show me cases which support X" is just asking for hallucinatory trouble with many current LLMs.

Nonetheless, I submit that hallucinations are, by definition, "incorrect behavior" and not merely "weak accuracy."

Now, nondeterminism could certainly be useful to the lawyer, in that it could help an LLM make connections that LexisNexis might not have in their database. So asking an LLM for help with legal issues is theoretically not an insane thing to do, but the results need to be checked very carefully.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我想说，[非确定性是否不好]是由你正在解决的问题明确定义的。

当然。 您给出了一个可以发挥作用的示例。 另一个例子是 PCB 或芯片布局引擎。

该特定域（布局）是 NP 完全的。 你永远不会对最佳布局进行详尽的蛮力搜索， 但...

随后您可以轻松检查生成的布局是否满足您的所有验收标准。

成功利用非确定性获得良好结果的另一个例子是将遗传算法应用于天线设计等领域。

这是有效的，因为 (a) 你有一个相对便宜的体能测试； (b) 与真正的进化一样，工作设计与随机引入突变的结合通常最终会产生出色的结果。

据推测，如果您应用遗传算法技术来创建 LLM 提示，您也可以获得良好的结果，但就代币而言，这可能很快就会变得昂贵。

因此，我们只能让人们半随机地修改提示，以尝试调整结果。

当它起作用时，它会是惊人的。 当它不起作用时，它就像一堵砖墙。

我喜欢你的“令人尴尬的不确定性”术语，但我有些不同意：

> 那么“错误”将被视为“准确性差”而不是“崩溃”或“不正确的行为”，

当一名律师要求法学硕士引用支持他立场的案例时，他可以说是在做一些愚蠢的事情，因为嵌入诸如“向我展示支持 X 的案例”之类的断言只会给许多当前的法学硕士带来幻觉的麻烦。

尽管如此，我认为，根据定义，幻觉是“不正确的行为”，而不仅仅是“准确性差”。

现在，非确定性肯定对律师有用，因为它可以帮助法学硕士建立 LexisNexis 数据库中可能没有的联系。 因此，向法学硕士寻求法律问题上的帮助理论上并不是一件疯狂的事情，但结果需要非常仔细地检查。

children.author: wseqyrku
children.textRawHtml:
I think I made an implicit distinction there that almost always get overlooked in these discussions, between runtime inference (when you run a transformer in your product) and offline inference (where you use AI in any ways anytime before that).

Chip design, etc would go to the latter category which is actually a huge space for innovation regardless of how you do it, while I was focusing more on the former which I think a lot of discussion seems to be concerned about.

Now if you are "running" your "generated code" that's where people will have strong opinions because it conflates the two completely different processes in the worst way possible.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为我在那里做了一个隐含的区别，在这些讨论中，运行时推断（当您在产品中运行变压器时）和离线推断（您在此之前的任何时间以任何方式使用人工智能）之间几乎总是被忽视。

芯片设计等将属于后一类，无论你如何做，这实际上都是一个巨大的创新空间，而我更关注前者，我认为很多讨论似乎都在关注前者。

现在，如果您正在“运行”“生成的代码”，那么人们就会有强烈的意见，因为它以最糟糕的方式混淆了两个完全不同的过程。

children.author: archagon
children.textRawHtml: More than that: it's not an abstraction layer jump, because AI is not an abstraction.
children.textZhHtml: 不仅如此：它不是抽象层的跳跃，因为人工智能不是抽象。

children.author: OptionOfT
children.textRawHtml:
I agree, but having abstractions allow us to learn things in a system that are broadly applied.

This is out the door for AI generated systems. Unless extreme care is taken there is is no consistency in these new-found codebases. New paradigms are introduced left and right, because in the eye of the LLM, and the prompter, they worked.

It didn't matter that the same pattern was repeated 37 times in a slightly different manner.

So your knowledge now is no longer portable.

It used to be that you could look at code, and ask why? And usually (not always) the answer was something like: 'I tried x, y, and z, and those didn't work', either by past experience or current experimentation. (we could argue if the current experimentation should be documented).

But for LLMs they put in something complex for no other reason than that it does what is requested. Reading a string from a valid source byte by byte until you hit \0 is valid, and it'll work. But if you take a step back at, read the API docs of what you're consuming, and then consider: if the API says X, why am I testing whether that's correct?
children.textZhHtml:
我同意，但是抽象可以让我们在广泛应用的系统中学习东西。

这对于人工智能生成的系统来说是不可能的。除非非常小心，否则这些新发现的代码库不会保持一致性。新的范例被左右引入，因为在法学硕士和提示者的眼中，它们是有效的。

即使相同的模式以稍微不同的方式重复 37 次也没关系。

所以你的知识现在不再是可移植的。

过去你可以查看代码，然后问为什么？通常（并非总是）答案是这样的：“我尝试了 x、y 和 z，但这些都不起作用”，无论是根据过去的经验还是当前的实验。 （我们可能会争论是否应该记录当前的实验）。

但对于法学硕士来说，他们投入一些复杂的东西没有其他原因，只是为了满足要求。从有效源逐字节读取字符串，直到您点击 \0 为止是有效的，并且它会起作用。但是，如果您退后一步，阅读您正在使用的内容的 API 文档，然后考虑：如果 API 说 X，为什么我要测试这是否正确？

children.author: Melatonic
children.textRawHtml:
I'm not sure that's true - you may be over generalizing

Probably not many here know the processes needed to turn sand into silicon and the expertise needed for hand grinding fancy lenses used in lithography. But we do know those things are needed and the approximate philosophical concepts behind these needs.

I think the danger comes when or if we fully automate a lot of the low level infrastructure tasks to the point where future generations do not even have the conceptual framework for how the tech they use is created
children.textZhHtml:
我不确定这是真的 - 你可能过于概括

这里可能没有多少人知道将沙子变成硅所需的过程以及手工研磨光刻中使用的精美镜片所需的专业知识。但我们确实知道这些东西是需要的，以及这些需求背后的大致哲学概念。

我认为，当我们完全自动化许多低级基础设施任务时，危险就会出现，以至于后代甚至不知道如何创建他们使用的技术的概念框架

children.author: ryandrake
children.textRawHtml: In 50 years, Gen Z will be retired, and they're going to be complaining: "The new generations don't know how anything works! They don't know how to enter in a prompt or optimize token usage. They don't even remember what keyboards are! They just Neropath into the UltraMind and synthesize facts into their neurons without even putting them to words or reading anything. We have lost something critical!"
children.textZhHtml: 50 年后，Z 世代将退休，他们会抱怨：“新一代不知道任何东西是如何运作的！他们不知道如何输入提示或优化令牌使用。他们甚至不记得键盘是什么！他们只是将 Neropath 输入 UltraMind，将事实合成到他们的神经元中，甚至没有将它们写成文字或阅读任何内容。我们失去了一些重要的东西！”

children.author: munk-a
children.textRawHtml:
Exactly, I find this sort of nostalgizing to be unhelpful. I am not _that_ old but I did cut my teeth on C++ and learned about manual memory management, programming language design and had a few (even at the time) esoteric courses about assembly language. I am not assembly fluent and have no desire to be - but getting an understanding of how higher level code compiles into lower level assembly/machine statements is invaluable to get a sense of how performance can be manipulated and the how impactful it can be to do "silly" things like unroll loops to minimize the number of instructions needed for some higher level operation.

Issac Newton/Bernard of Chartres said we stand on the shoulders of giants and that allows us to see further afield but it also loses the detail of the ground beneath us - modern folks will no longer have the broad expertise that someone struggling through building a computer from transistors would gain through overcoming that[1], but we should learn about it academically to garner the bits of knowledge that remain salient. It is incredibly useful when experts in these highly specific fields have the communication skills and knowledge to share what is relevant to others without overwhelming them[2] with detail.

1. Real modern consumer grade computers are far too complex to understand fully without dedicating your life (or a significant portion of it) solely to that. It is more pragmatic to allow specialization and small realm expertise instead.

2. Of course, if you want to geek out and learn the minutiae that's awesome - but we can only geek so much in our limited time here.
children.textZhHtml:
确实，我觉得这种怀旧是没有帮助的。 我还没有那么老，但我确实在 C++ 上崭露头角，了解了手动内存管理、编程语言设计，并学习了一些（即使在当时）有关汇编语言的深奥课程。 我不熟悉汇编，也不想精通汇编，但了解较高级别的代码如何编译成较低级别的汇编/机器语句对于了解如何操纵性能以及执行诸如展开循环之类的“愚蠢”事情以最大程度地减少某些较高级别操作所需的指令数量的影响是非常宝贵的。

沙特尔的伊萨克·牛顿/伯纳德说，我们站在巨人的肩膀上，这让我们看得更远，但也失去了我们脚下地面的细节——现代人将不再拥有那些努力用晶体管建造计算机的人通过克服这一点而获得的广泛专业知识[1]，但我们应该从学术上学习它，以获取仍然突出的知识点。 当这些高度特定领域的专家拥有沟通技巧和知识来分享与他人相关的内容而不用细节压垮他们[2]时，这是非常有用的。

1. 真正的现代消费级计算机过于复杂，如果不将您的生活（或大部分时间）奉献给它，就无法完全理解。 更务实的是允许专业化和小领域的专业知识。

2. 当然，如果你想探索并了解细节，那就太棒了 - 但我们在有限的时间内只能探索这么多。

children.author: xpct
children.textRawHtml:
> Generally speaking, we know how the level of abstraction that we were born with works

I'd say it's generally true that the majority of jobs of an era deal with a similar level of abstraction, and that's why most people stay on it. However, I frame this as being born with technical debt, and it's my obligation as an engineer to understand what the previous generations have built, and where it makes sense for me to work, directionally.
children.textZhHtml:
> 一般来说，我们知道我们与生俱来的抽象水平是如何运作的

我想说，一个时代的大多数工作都处理类似的抽象水平，这通常是正确的，这就是大多数人坚持下去的原因。然而，我认为这是与生俱来的技术债务，作为一名工程师，我有义务了解前几代人所构建的内容，以及对我来说有方向性的工作有意义的地方。

children.author: thinking_cactus
children.textRawHtml:
I think it's more that "Some human some time has known how it works", not that "Any given human knows how it (all) works.".

But yea this glosses over a bit trial-and-error designs and, so to speak, "genetic optimization" kinds of designs where we just try random stuff and say "Hey, this works. Not sure why, but it works.".
children.textZhHtml:
我认为更多的是“某些人在某些时候已经知道它是如何工作的”，而不是“任何特定的人都知道它（全部）是如何工作的”。

But yea this glosses over a bit trial-and-error designs and, so to speak, "genetic optimization" kinds of designs where we just try random stuff and say "Hey, this works. Not sure why, but it works.".

children.author: vitally3643
children.textRawHtml:
There was a period of a century or more where steam engine design parameters were determined by experiment and gut feel. The physics of fluid dynamics required to explain why a design is optimal just did not exist yet.

There's a lot of places in history where engineering far outpaced the science required to explain it.
children.textZhHtml:
在一个世纪或更长时间的时间里，蒸汽机的设计参数是通过实验和直觉确定的。需要用流体力学物理学来解释 为什么 最佳设计只是还不存在。

历史上有很多地方工程远远超过了解释它所需的科学。

children.author: budman1
children.textRawHtml:
If your design work is not based upon science and mathematics, it is not engineering.

Not saying engineering is required to build or improve a product; it is not.

"try this" is not engineering.
children.textZhHtml:
如果你的设计工作不是基于科学和数学，那么它就不是工程。

并不是说构建或改进产品需要工程技术；它不是。

“试试这个”不是工程。

children.author: neerajsi
children.textRawHtml: I disagree. For example, the Wright brothers did air foil engineering empirically without full knowledge of the underlying fluid mechanics. Early and innovative engineering will often be less based on existing scientific or mathematical knowledge, while still using some of the methods. It's still absolutely defined as engineering.
children.textZhHtml: 我不同意。例如，莱特兄弟在没有充分了解基础流体力学的情况下凭经验进行了翼片工程。早期的创新工程通常较少基于现有的科学或数学知识，但仍使用某些方法。它仍然绝对被定义为工程。

children.author: drdexebtjl
children.textRawHtml:
Of course it is. The science comes from reproducing what you tried and getting the same results.

You may not have a good enough model for this observation to allow you to predict the results of other starting conditions, but it’s a model nonetheless, one that narrowly applies to the conditions you tried.
children.textZhHtml:
当然是。科学来自于重复你尝试过的事情并得到相同的结果。

您可能没有足够好的模型来进行此观察，以允许您预测其他起始条件的结果，但它仍然是一个模型，一个狭隘地适用于您尝试的条件的模型。

children.author: ranger_danger
children.textRawHtml:
> "try this" is not engineering.

It was always engineering. You're making the assumption that their theories are not rooted in anything coherent at all, but almost always this is not the case. Usually a "what if we..." thought is based on other knowledge, and there's absolutely nothing wrong with trial and error... this is what research and experimentation, areas of science, are all about.
children.textZhHtml:
> “试试这个”不是工程。

它始终是工程。你假设他们的理论根本不植根于任何连贯的东西，但几乎总是事实并非如此。通常，“如果我们……会怎么样”的想法是基于其他知识，而尝试和错误绝对没有错……这就是研究和实验、科学领域的全部内容。

children.author: spacedcowboy
children.textRawHtml:

children.author: VorpalWay
children.textRawHtml:
When I took software focused computer engineering around 2010, we still had courses that took us all the way down to transistors and even the physics of P and N junctions and how that applies to CMOS. (And even some basic analog electronics.)

Did I end up an expert at those layers? Of course not, but I know the basics and I know enough that if I need to I know where to start learning more. Just like I wasn't a C++ or hard realtime expert after university either, but now a decade and a half later I am pretty good at those (and a bunch of other skills that ended up relevant to my line of work).

Basically, none of the layers are "magic" to me. Even if I don't know the details of it, I know the general principle and I know I could learn more if I need it.

(I think you naturally end up an expert at the layer(s) you work in, and the knowledge tapers off as you go down (or up) the stack. For example, I know a fair bit about how the CPU works (cache coherency, pipeline stalls etc), I can passably read x86 assembly, etc. Because they affect the layer I work at (hard realtime systems C++ and now also Rust). I know far less about web dev than hardware.)
children.textZhHtml:
当我在 2010 年左右学习以软件为中心的计算机工程时，我们仍然有课程将我们一路深入到晶体管，甚至 P 和 N 结的物理原理以及如何将其应用于 CMOS。 （甚至还有一些基本的模拟电子设备。）

我最终成为这些层面的专家吗？当然不是，但我知道基础知识，而且我知道得足够多，如果我需要的话，我知道从哪里开始学习更多。就像我不是 C++ 或硬实时人员一样 专家 大学毕业后，但现在十五年后，我非常擅长这些（以及一堆最终与我的工作相关的其他技能）。

基本上，没有任何一层对我来说是“神奇的”。即使我不知道它的细节，我也知道一般原理，并且我知道如果需要的话我可以了解更多。

（我认为你自然而然地会成为你工作所在层的专家，并且随着你向下（或向上）堆栈，知识会逐渐减少。例如，我对 CPU 的工作原理（缓存一致性、管道停顿等）有相当多的了解，我可以通过阅读 x86 程序集等。因为它们影响我工作的层（硬实时系统 C++，现在还包括 Rust）。我知道 远的 与网络开发相关的内容较少，与硬件相关的内容较少。）

children.author: VorpalWay
children.textRawHtml:
I partially disagree. I know the basic high level concept of all those. Would I be able to reproduce state of the art results on my own? Obviously not.

But a core part of the engineer or scientist mindset is curiosity for the sake of curiosity. Just the fact that I don't know something is enough reason for me to poke at it or otherwise learn more. Same reason I still take apart broken electronics as an adult and try to find the fault (and sometimes even repair it).

By the way, mining silicon is particularly easy: it's basically sand. The difficult part is purifying it, especially to the levels needed for modern nm scale chips.

A more useful question than making high end silicon would be: could you with reasonable tooling reproduce basic electric components? I'm talking things like light bulbs, resistors, generators, perhaps capacitors even? Just the basics crappy versions, not modern highly optimised surface mount components. And I think the anwer is yes (for me personally) if I had access to metal wire and sheet stock and industrial revolution era tools.
children.textZhHtml:
我部分不同意。我知道所有这些的基本高级概念。我能自己重现最先进的结果吗？显然不是。

但工程师或科学家心态的核心部分是为了好奇而好奇。我不知道某件事这一事实就足以让我有理由去研究它或以其他方式了解更多信息。同样的原因，我仍然在成年后拆开损坏的电子产品并尝试找到故障（有时甚至修复它）。

顺便说一句，开采硅特别容易：它基本上是沙子。困难的部分是纯化它，尤其是达到现代纳米级芯片所需的水平。

比制造高端硅更有用的问题是：你能用合理的工具复制基本的电子元件吗？我说的是灯泡、电阻器、发电机，甚至电容器？只是基础的蹩脚版本，而不是现代高度优化的表面贴装组件。我认为，如果我能够获得金属线材和板材以及工业革命时代的工具，答案是肯定的（对我个人而言）。

children.author: dranudin
children.textRawHtml:
I was once in a museum in Bolzano, Italy. And they had the ice man mummy there (we call him Ötzi). He died like 5000years ago. And his "axe" was made of copper from some mine very far away. So even this guy probably did not know how everything he had was made. 
There is a theory that he was probably very rich. So maybe less rich people were more in touch with what they owned. But still I found it fascinating that even so far back people relied on technology and materials that they didn't really know about.
children.textZhHtml:
我曾经去过意大利博尔扎诺的一家博物馆。他们那里有冰人木乃伊（我们叫他奥茨）。他大约5000年前就去世了。他的“斧头”是用来自很远的矿井的铜制成的。所以连这个家伙恐怕也不知道他所拥有的一切是如何炼成的。 
有一种理论认为他可能非常富有。因此，也许不太富有的人更关心他们所拥有的东西。但我仍然觉得很有趣的是，即使在很早以前，人们仍然依赖他们并不真正了解的技术和材料。

children.author: alex_c
children.textRawHtml:
The one thing I appreciated about my Computer Engineering undergrad - and it took me a few years to fully appreciate it - is that yes, we did cover those levels.

The first two years were shared with Electrical Engineering. The second two years started to specialize towards Computer Engineering topics.

* Physics and chemistry.

* Circuits.

* Transistors.

* Logic gates.

* FPGAs.

* Assembly.

* Compilers.

* CPU and hardware design.

* Operating systems.

* Networking layers.

* Programming languages.

* Computer graphics.

Did I master all of the above - absolutely not. I loved some of them, struggled with others. Generally the cut-off for how my brain works is logic gates, I was never strong at the levels below that.

But we did cover them, and I could honestly say I had at least a rough understanding and mental map of everything that happens inside a computer from the point where it's plugged into an outlet, to the point where pixels show up on the screen.
children.textZhHtml:
我对我的计算机工程本科生感到欣赏的一件事 - 我花了几年时间才完全欣赏它 - 是的，我们确实涵盖了这些级别。

前两年与电气工程共享。第二年开始专注于计算机工程主题。

* 物理和化学。

* 电路。

* 晶体管。

* 逻辑门。

* FPGA。

* 集会。

* 编译器。

* CPU 和硬件设计。

* 操作系统。

* 网络层。

* 编程语言。

* 计算机图形学。

我是否掌握了以上所有内容——绝对没有。 我爱他们中的一些人，也与另一些人斗争。 一般来说，我的大脑如何运作的界限 是 逻辑门，我在低于它的水平上从来都不强。

但我们确实涵盖了它们，而且我可以诚实地说我至少有一个 粗糙的 从计算机插入插座到像素显示在屏幕上的整个过程的理解和思维导图。

children.author: esikich
children.textRawHtml: What's absurd is thinking because you took a logic class and made a flip flop 30 years ago that that is the ground floor and that it means you "understand how it all works". You're not building a CPU from logic gates and you don't know how it works. If I put you or OP in a room for a year I highly doubt you could build an 8 bit Atari-like CPU from scratch. I worked with wires and logic too but I'm not arrogant enough to say that I know how it all works.
children.textZhHtml: 荒谬的是，因为你在 30 年前上过逻辑课并制作了一个人字拖，所以你就认为 那 是一楼 和 这意味着您“了解这一切是如何运作的”。你不是从逻辑门构建 CPU 并且你 不 知道它是如何工作的。如果我把你或 OP 放在一个房间里一年，我非常怀疑你能否从头开始构建一个类似 Atari 的 8 位 CPU。我也研究过电线和逻辑，但我还没有傲慢地说我知道这一切是如何运作的。

children.author: vladms
children.textRawHtml:
Don't you think there is a difference from "knowing how it works" and "reproducing every aspect of it at the level of the state of the art" ?

Also, your example seems flawed if you restrict to a certain product. Can I build a compiler from scratch? Yes. Can I reproduce in a year a compiler with LLVM/GCC performance level? No. Can I build a compiler from scratch in a year from a room if I need to starting mining from metals, building transistors, then building the first assembler and then implementing the compiler? You can imagine the answer.
children.textZhHtml:
您不认为“知道它是如何工作的”和“以最先进的水平复制它的各个方面”之间有区别吗？

另外，如果您限制于某种产品，您的示例似乎有缺陷。我可以从头开始构建编译器吗？是的。我可以在一年内重现具有 LLVM/GCC 性能级别的编译器吗？如果我需要开始开采金属、构建晶体管、然后构建第一个汇编器，然后实现编译器，我可以在一年内从头开始在一个房间里构建一个编译器吗？你可以想象答案。

children.author: esikich
children.textRawHtml: Not really, at least not in this discussion. An assembly programmer gets logic gates, a C programmer gets assembly, etc etc. And each one can say to the one above "well you don't really get it if you can't bang out some meaningful work". Otherwise idk what we're even talking about here. Having a vague understanding of something isn't "knowing how it works".
children.textZhHtml: 并非如此，至少在本次讨论中并非如此。汇编程序员获得逻辑门，C 程序员获得汇编，等等。每个人都可以对上面的人说“如果你不能完成一些有意义的工作，你就不会真正理解它”。否则我不知道我们在这里谈论什么。对某事物有模糊的理解并不是“知道它是如何工作的”。

children.author: lelanthran
children.textRawHtml:
> If I put you or OP in a room for a year I highly doubt you could build an 8 bit Atari-like CPU from scratch.

I believe I can, depending on what "from scratch" means. With nothing but transistors, resistors, inductors, coils, capacitors, I can probably do apretty poor general-purpose CPU. Maybe something like a stack-based ISA or, if I had more time, an accumulator-based processor like a 6502, but with an 8-bit bus.

If you're going to ask me to create transistors themselves, well I believe that needs specialised equipment.
children.textZhHtml:
> 如果我把你或 OP 在一个房间里待一年，我非常怀疑你能否从头开始构建一个类似 Atari 的 8 位 CPU。

我相信我可以，这取决于“从头开始”的含义。除了晶体管、电阻器、电感器、线圈、电容器之外什么都没有，我可能可以做很差的通用CPU。也许是基于堆栈的 ISA，或者，如果我有更多时间，可以是基于累加器的处理器，例如 6502，但具有 8 位总线。

如果你要我自己制造晶体管，我相信这需要专门的设备。

Yes, thats exactly what uni is for.. To learn all those previous centuries of stuff before you can either start contributing to the corpus yourself, or go get a job (Where you will pick up more immediately useful stuff)

We really dont understand how AI is working, even the earliest "genetic algorithms" could be incomprehensible, but computer systems in general, they're not really that complicated.. its like an audio mixing desk..it looks insanely complicated until you realise it's just the same few knobs repeated many times for many channels. comments.author: tor0ugh
comments.textRawHtml:
It is no small feat to put in words that we are losing something almost as quickly as we are gaining something. The undertone, despite leaning into nostalgia boils down to losing control and this uneasiness I feel growing daily. It is already shocking to a certain degree seeing very young people not being able to use a computer in the narrow sense because all they ever learned was touch interfaces and apps. Curated content, curated interfaces - everything that resembles some kind of hardship ironed out in thousand steps of iterations to appease the market which means the lowest common denominator.

But I also see that the people who can create the absolute most and the good things and the working things and the maintainable things nowadays are the people that have gained a tool, but not lost the knowledge of the medium we are using it on because we are tied to this old world so perfectly put under the spotlight in this blog post.
comments.textZhHtml:
可以说，我们失去一些东西的速度几乎和我们获得一些东西的速度一样快，这可不是一件小事。尽管倾向于怀旧，但其基调归结为失去控制，而且我感到这种不安与日俱增。看到非常年轻人无法使用狭义的计算机，在某种程度上已经令人震惊，因为他们所学到的只是触摸界面和应用程序。精心策划的内容、精心策划的界面——一切类似于某种困难的东西都经过数千次迭代来解决，以安抚市场，这意味着最低的共同点。

但我也看到，当今能够创造出绝对最多、美好事物、有用事物和可维护事物的人是那些获得了工具的人，但没有失去我们正在使用它的媒介的知识，因为我们与这个旧世界联系在一起，因此在这篇博文中完美地置于聚光灯下。

children.author: apsurd
children.textRawHtml:
The financialization of everything is what's ruining everything. In the computer and internet realm, there's warm nostalgia from hours spent tinkering, building one's own PC, reformatting C drives due to malware, searching for "snippets" to add a forum or pimp a myspace page. But inevitably the money incentives come to dominate. It's all of our doing, we all dream of a better life to put it charitably.

Now everything is a means to a commercial end. Tinkering for fun and knowledge just isn't profitable. And it matters less and less what each our stance is on money and capital if the people that optimize for money and capital gobble up all the money and capital. Of all that's going on, the wealth gap is what's most troubling to me, closely followed because it's closely related is "post truth". I think post-truth is roughly caused by the fact that people are happy to believe what they want to believe toward some commercialized and/or idealogical end. You're much more likely to hate and blame your neighbor when you look around and you're the one not doing too well.
children.textZhHtml:
一切事物的金融化正在毁掉一切。在计算机和互联网领域，人们会因花几个小时修修补补、组装自己的电脑、因恶意软件而重新格式化 C 驱动器、搜索“片段”来添加论坛或拉皮条 myspace 页面而产生温暖的怀旧之情。但金钱激励不可避免地占据主导地位。这都是我们所做的，慈善地说，我们都梦想着过上更好的生活。

现在一切都是达到商业目的的手段。为了乐趣和知识而修补是没有利润的。如果那些优化金钱和资本的人吞噬了所有的金钱和资本，那么我们对金钱和资本的立场就越来越不重要了。在所有正在发生的事情中，贫富差距是最令我困扰的，也是紧随其后的，因为它与“后真相”密切相关。我认为后真相大致是由于人们乐于相信他们想相信的事情造成的 朝向 某种商业化和/或意识形态的目的。当你环顾四周，发现自己做得不太好时，你更有可能憎恨和责备你的邻居。

children.author: footydude
children.textRawHtml:
In my mind, 'tinkering for fun and knowledge' was never meant to be profitable - if you're tinkering with the express aim of making a profit i'm not sure you're really 'tinkering' so much as you're trying to create a product/service/output for someone else to pay for?

There's absolutely nothing wrong with doing that - I'm just not sure the 'ethos' of tinkering has anything to do with trying to make money and is usually reserved for describing someone playing about with something for their own enjoyment/fun with no desire to make money.

Now, of course some people did find that their tinkerings were able to make them money, but I think at its base it's a term I'd tend to say implies doing something for fun/for themselves, rather than doing it for profit?

In my experience there's still plenty of people out there tinkering just for their own personal satisfaction, but of course there's almost certain a whole load more people out there 'tinkering' to try make a profit.
children.textZhHtml:
在我看来，“修补乐趣和知识”是 绝不 意味着盈利 - 如果你正在修改盈利的明确目标，我不确定你是否真的在“修改”，以至于你试图创建一个产品/服务/输出供其他人支付？

这样做绝对没有错——我只是不确定修修补补的“精神”与试图赚钱有什么关系，并且通常用来描述某人为了自己的享受/乐趣而玩弄某样东西，而不是想赚钱。

现在，当然有些人 做过 发现他们的修补能够让他们赚钱，但我认为从根本上来说，我倾向于说这个术语意味着为了乐趣/为自己做某事，而不是为了利润而做？

根据我的经验，仍然有很多人只是为了自己的个人满足而进行修补，但当然几乎可以肯定有更多的人在“修补”以试图盈利。

children.author: apsurd
children.textRawHtml:
Agreed, I'd say tinkering for profit has become the defacto optimization and tinkering for its own sake is less fashionable because it commands no attention. and everyone is obsessed with the modern currency: attention.

To be clear, I'm seeing this as an observed phenomenon, not that everyone made up their mind that they hate tinkering and love money. I just think it's getting really really hard to exist in the world as a normal person when the entire human collective is getting pumped commercialized hyper-media from all angles at all times now 100X'd by genAI bots. It's really exhausting and so you either opt-in to the game, monetize your now AI side-hustle to pay the rent, or opt-out and live in the woods. and get packages delivered by Amazon. heh.
children.textZhHtml:
同意，我想说，为了利润而进行修补已经成为事实上的优化，而为了其本身而进行修补则不太流行，因为它不会引起人们的注意。每个人都痴迷于现代货币：注意力。

需要明确的是，我认为这是一个观察到的现象，并不是每个人都下定决心讨厌修修补补而热爱金钱。我只是认为，当整个人类集体从各个角度、任何时候都被 genAI 机器人 100 倍地注入商业化超媒体时，作为一个正常人在世界上生存真的变得非常困难。这真的很累人，所以你要么选择加入游戏，用你现在的人工智能副业赚钱来支付房租，要么选择退出并住在树林里。并获取亚马逊递送的包裹。呵呵。

children.author: overgard
children.textRawHtml: The real problem is the philosophy that arose in the 1970s that "maximizing shareholder value" is the point of business. As a society, we've become obsessed with optimizing this one variable at the expense of all others and "growth" has become a religion. At it's core, the most important thing we should be optimizing for is "does this business improve the world while turning a robust and sustainable revenue stream" not "how much money can we make for investors". The latter will always optimize for incredible wealth disparity, enshittification, and mostly useless people like Elon Musk becoming stupidly wealthy because it's basically impossible for them to lose money no matter how bad their ideas are (once you're a certain level of rich, your passive income is just immense). I'm not saying shareholders/investors shouldn't be considered, but it can't be the only or the most important metric. We've completely lost the plot on the social and market purpose of a corporation.
children.textZhHtml: 真正的问题是 20 世纪 70 年代出现的理念，即“股东价值最大化”是商业的重点。作为一个社会，我们痴迷于以牺牲所有其他变量为代价来优化这一变量，“增长”已成为一种宗教。从本质上讲，我们应该优化的最重要的事情是“这项业务是否能够改善世界，同时带来强劲且可持续的收入来源”，而不是“我们能为投资者赚多少钱”。后者总是会优化令人难以置信的贫富差距、enshittiification，以及像埃隆·马斯克这样的无用之人变得愚蠢的富有，因为无论他们的想法多么糟糕，他们基本上都不可能赔钱（一旦你达到一定程度的富有，你的被动收入就是巨大的）。我并不是说股东/投资者不应该被考虑，但它不能是唯一或最重要的指标。我们完全失去了对公司的社会和市场目的的理解。

children.author: PaulDavisThe1st
children.textRawHtml:
> It is already shocking to a certain degree seeing very young people not being able to use a vehicle in the narrow sense because all they ever learned were the mechanical controls of the so-called automobile.

We could do this forever.
children.textZhHtml:
> 看到非常年轻的人无法使用狭义上的车辆，这已经在某种程度上令人震惊，因为他们所学到的只是所谓汽车的机械控制。

我们可以永远这样做。

children.author: tines
children.textRawHtml: I think there's a fallacy where someone points out one instance of a larger trend which will, when taken to its logical progression, lead to an undesired effect; and then someone attempts to rebut the claim by pointing out that the trend has existed before and the undesired effect hasn't happened yet, so any concern is nugatory. I'd call it the grippery slope fallacy, complement to the slippery one: we haven't fallen down the slope yet, so we can't fall down it. What if an individual instance of ignorance is acceptable because people still need to have understanding in other areas, but if all understanding everywhere is eliminated then we all suffer?
children.textZhHtml: 我认为存在一种谬论，即有人指出了一种更大趋势的一个实例，当按照其逻辑发展进行分析时，会导致意想不到的效果；然后有人试图反驳这一说法，指出这种趋势以前就已经存在，而且尚未发生不良影响，因此任何担忧都是无效的。我把它称为“抓地力斜坡谬误”，是对滑坡谬误的补充：我们还没有从斜坡上掉下来，所以我们不能从斜坡上掉下来。如果个别的无知是可以接受的，因为人们仍然需要在其他领域有理解，但如果所有地方的理解都被消除了，那么我们都会受苦呢？

children.author: switchbak
children.textRawHtml:
Absolutely - you used to have to control the richness of the fuel mixture manually. You used to have to crank it to start it, manually interact with a clutch to shift gears, etc.

I appreciate the tactile joy of interacting with simple systems like those, but most times I just want to get where I'm going. Freeing my attention from those tasks allows me to pay more attention to the (inattentive) drivers around me, and try my best to not die.

Eventually a computer will handle driving for most of us, and we can lament about all the things we've lost there too. If you zoom out, most of us don't have an in-depth understanding of how an entire city works (power, garbage, sewage, maintenance, public services, politics, etc), and couldn't coordinate the various activities to keep it running if we had to. We live in towers of abstraction.
children.textZhHtml:
绝对可以——您过去必须手动控制燃油混合物的浓度。您过去必须摇动它才能启动它，手动与离合器交互以换档等等。

我很欣赏与这些简单系统交互的触觉乐趣，但大多数时候我只想到达我要去的地方。将我的注意力从这些任务中解放出来，让我能够更多地关注周围（不专心的）司机，并尽力不死。

最终，计算机将为我们大多数人处理驾驶问题，我们也可以为我们失去的所有东西而哀叹。如果你缩小范围，我们大多数人都无法深入了解整个城市的运作方式（电力、垃圾、污水、维护、公共服务、政治等），并且如果必须的话，也无法协调各种活动以保持其运行。我们生活在抽象的塔中。

children.author: apsurd
children.textRawHtml:
My read is that there does seem a clear difference between simple -> advanced machines vs simple -> "smart" machines. Nearly every smart machine is bullshit enshitification-in-waiting. Rent-seeking in-waiting. Smart tvs, smart cars with touch-screens. some would argue apple products. These things proclaim advancements but what they really do is black-box and dumb everything down to the lowest common denominator, then quite literally impose control over the air, and shove ads to you.

I'm all for just getting to where I need to go by using the appropriate tool, like a reliable car. But no not if it means foregoing the liberty of other options.
children.textZhHtml:
我的理解是，简单 -> 高级机器与简单 -> “智能”机器之间似乎确实存在明显差异。几乎所有智能机器都在等待着胡说八道。寻租等待。智能电视、带触摸屏的智能汽车。有些人会争论苹果产品。这些东西宣告了进步，但它们真正做的是黑匣子，将一切简化到最低的公分母，然后完全控制空气，并向你推送广告。

我完全赞成使用适当的工具（例如可靠的汽车）到达我需要去的地方。但如果这意味着放弃其他选择的自由，那就不行了。

children.author: switchbak
children.textRawHtml:
I think the dumbing down of things is a closely related yet adjacent issue. And I too absolutely despise the enshitification you’re describing.

I drove a BMW rental recently, and I swear it felt like I was driving around a toy. Such obvious HCI usability concerns, even my wife (non techie) was appalled and able to call out many obvious and stupid design decisions. It felt like a vibe coded car. A vision of the future, no doubt.

我最近开着一辆租来的宝马，我发誓感觉就像我在开着一个玩具。如此明显的人机交互可用性问题，就连我的妻子（非技术人员）也感到震惊，并能够指出许多明显且愚蠢的设计决策。感觉就像一辆氛围编码的汽车。毫无疑问，这是对未来的愿景。

Programming languages, UNIX, debuggers aren’t going anywhere. There is more to computing than what your boss demands and what is hyped on tech forums.

In fact I believe the indie/handmade scene will grow substantial if not boom, even if just as a hobby for most. Showing what you have made with your blood sweat and tears will elicit more praise and delight when you could have just asked a machine to do it all along.

comments.author: Stefan-H
comments.textRawHtml:
There was a sweet spot with computer technologies for some decades where hobbyists could afford to experiment and even push the envelope in the nascent field of computing - similar to genetic radiation, many niches were formed and rapidly filled. The computing biome has evolved to the point where most entities are not operating at the low-level abstractions that were once the only means of interacting with the computing environment, instead they operate now at the highest levels of abstraction we are capable, so called "natural language".

"The difficulty was the knowledge. You came to know that machine the way you come to know anything that pushes back. The resistance was the whole medium. You only ever know the things that you can lose to."

We who grew up in this era formed a hands-on engineer's knowledge of these systems, built from experience and practice, learning these layers of abstraction as the bleeding edge developed. Many these days have entered into a world where there are easy answers abound, they just might not be right, and one has to gauge how much they care about correctness.
comments.textZhHtml:
几十年来，计算机技术一直处于最佳状态，爱好者们可以在新兴的计算领域进行实验，甚至挑战极限——与基因辐射类似，许多利基市场已经形成并迅速被填补。计算生物群系已经发展到这样的程度：大多数实体不再在低级抽象上运行，而低级抽象曾经是与计算环境交互的唯一手段，而是在我们能够实现的最高抽象级别上运行，即所谓的“自然语言”。

“困难在于知识。你对机器的了解就像你对任何阻碍的事物的了解一样。阻力是整个媒介。你只知道那些你可能会输掉的东西。”

在这个时代长大的我们，在经验和实践的基础上形成了对这些系统的实践工程师的知识，随着前沿技术的发展，学习这些抽象层。如今，许多人已经进入了一个充满简单答案的世界，但它们可能并不正确，人们必须衡量他们对正确性的关心程度。

It was easy, back in the day, to learn a ton (even assembler) on a system like that, and I feel like there's some value in rewinding the clock back to that point, forking the experience from there, and seeing what a new generation of kids will cook up.
children.textZhHtml: 这绝对是真的，我们肯定再次需要类似的东西。我一直使用一个游戏（“农民被取代”）作为教孩子们 Python 的起点，但我越想它，我就越认为他们需要某种类似于旧 Apple //e 的硬件包，给他们足够的绳子来吊死自己。在过去，在这样的系统上学习大量内容（甚至是汇编程序）是很容易的，我觉得把时间倒回到那个点，从那里分叉经验，看看新一代的孩子会创造出什么东西是有一定价值的。

comments.author: steelframe
comments.textRawHtml:
I'm one of the greybeards who has the 2400 BAUD modem negotiation tone sequences emblazened in my neurons.

For a while I've been meaning to set up some Wireguard connections among some of my systems. Being as busy as I am with work and family, I've relinquished that to Tailscale for now.

Sure, I could have sat down and jumped through the hoops to get everything set up and working across my various hosts, including network routes, firewall rules, key pairs, systemd units, and so forth. But the "cheap and easy" alternative was right there and worked (except when it forces re-authentication).

With LLM agents, I was able to effortlessly analyze my existing network and produce tailored scripts to do precisely what I wanted. All I had to do was review the scripts for potential security issues and what not. Looking at the script, there are 3 or 4 specific tweaks that needed to be made to my network routing rules given my network topology. I could have read a few man pages and iterated on the script by hand to eventually get there after maybe an hour or two of futzing.

The availability and effectiveness of the agents is simply too tempting for me. I'm not sure what this means about my skillset, or if that even matters any more. I am fairly confident that, so long as my brain still works well enough, I'll always be able to RTFM and figure things like this out myself. At this rate I wonder whether my kids will have the same ability. And I also wonder how much that will matter.

Regardless, I'm still helping them figure things out the "old way" without over-reliance on LLMs. One thing I'm fairly certain about is that failure to develop problem-solving skills can only put them in a worse position in life, no matter how capable AI becomes.
comments.textZhHtml:
我是其中一位在我的神经元中刻有 2400 BAUD 调制解调器协商音序列的灰胡子老人。

一段时间以来，我一直想在我的一些系统之间建立一些 Wireguard 连接。由于我的工作和家庭都很忙，所以我暂时把它交给了 Tailscale。

当然，我可以坐下来，跳过障碍，让所有内容在我的各种主机上设置并运行，包括网络路由、防火墙规则、密钥对、systemd 单元等等。但“便宜又简单”的替代方案就在那里并且有效（除非它强制重新验证）。

有了法学硕士代理，我能够毫不费力地分析我现有的网络并生成定制的脚本来精确地完成我想要的事情。我所要做的就是检查脚本是否存在潜在的安全问题以及其他问题。查看脚本，根据我的网络拓扑，需要对我的网络路由规则进行 3 或 4 个特定调整。我本可以阅读一些手册页并手动迭代脚本，最终在经过一两个小时的努力后到达那里。

代理人的可用性和有效性对我来说太有吸引力了。我不确定这对我的技能意味着什么，或者这是否还重要。我相当有信心，只要我的大脑仍然运转良好，我就总是能够通过 RTFM 自己解决类似的问题。照这样下去，我想知道我的孩子是否也有同样的能力。我也想知道这有多重要。

无论如何，我仍在帮助他们以“旧方式”解决问题，而不过度依赖法学硕士。我相当确定的一件事是，无论人工智能变得多么有能力，未能培养解决问题的能力只会让他们在生活中处于更糟糕的境地。

children.author: spopejoy
children.textRawHtml:
> I could have read a few man pages and iterated on the script by hand to eventually get there after maybe an hour or two of futzing

I'm not even sure losing this method is such a bad thing. Tooling, build scripts, home networking -- any learning you gain from stumbling through man pages and trial-and-error at best results in some hard-won, single-purpose scripts, the knowledge will evaporate instantly b/c everything is so bespoke, "fix once and forget" until you have new gear and have to solve new problems years later.

Presenting a well-specified problem thoroughly to an LLM, then analyzing results and testing the output, is valid problem solving.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我本可以阅读一些手册页并手动迭代脚本，最终在经过一两个小时的努力后到达那里

I'm not even sure losing this method is such a bad thing. Tooling, build scripts, home networking -- any learning you gain from stumbling through man pages and trial-and-error at best results in some hard-won, single-purpose scripts, the knowledge will evaporate instantly b/c everything is so bespoke, "fix once and forget" until you have new gear and have to solve new problems years later.

向法学硕士彻底提出一个明确的问题，然后分析结果并测试输出，这是有效的问题解决方法。

children.author: switchbak
children.textRawHtml:
The thing is - everyone complains about AI stealing our attention and understanding. But you can just as easily use an LLM as a tool to gain a deeper understanding. It's just the default path for most folks is "Hey clanker, do the thing" rather than "Hello clanker, please tell me about how that thing works".

I've done at least a little of the latter, and it's amazing how underrated it is as an educational tool - especially for the solo individual.
children.textZhHtml:
问题是——每个人都抱怨人工智能窃取了我们的注意力和理解力。但您可以轻松地使用法学硕士作为获得更深入了解的工具。对于大多数人来说，默认路径是“嘿 clanker，做这件事”，而不是“你好 clanker，请告诉我那件事是如何工作的”。

I've done at least a little of the latter, and it's amazing how underrated it is as an educational tool - especially for the solo individual.

children.author: tines
children.textRawHtml:
And you can use a TV to read books, but nobody does.

Every technology has ways it can be used, and ways it wants to be used. This one wants to be used in a way that produces outcomes we won't like.
children.textZhHtml:
你可以用电视看书，但没人这样做。

每种技术都有它可以使用的方式，以及它想要使用的方式。这个的使用方式会产生我们不喜欢的结果。

children.author: switchbak
children.textRawHtml: That’s the “diets don’t work” line. I’m not denying that most people use AI for the path of least resistance, but I am saying that there’s more potential than just that, and it’s almost entirely overlooked in the broad debate.
children.textZhHtml: 这就是“节食不起作用”的说法。我并不否认大多数人使用人工智能是为了阻力最小的道路，但我想说的是，人工智能的潜力不止于此，而且在广泛的辩论中它几乎完全被忽视了。

children.author: tines
children.textRawHtml:
Bad counterexample, c.f. the current obesity epidemic and the popularity of GLP-1s.

I get what you're saying, it's just that I think what you're saying is not overlooked, it's simply insignificant to the debate about the effect of these tools on society. Your argument comes off to me like someone saying "everyone is complaining about how these cigarettes cause cancer, but you can just as easily use lit cigarettes to warm your hands on a cold day. This is what everyone's missing in this debate."

I don't think we can change each others' minds with talk, we'll just have to see how it plays out in the coming decades.
children.textZhHtml:
不好的反例，参见当前的肥胖流行和 GLP-1 的普及。

我明白你在说什么，只是我认为你所说的并没有被忽视，它对于关于这些工具对社会影响的争论来说根本无关紧要。你的论点对我来说就像有人说“每个人都在抱怨这些香烟如何导致癌症，但你可以在寒冷的日子里轻松地使用点燃的香烟来温暖你的手。这就是每个人在这场辩论中所缺少的。”

我不认为我们可以通过谈话来改变彼此的想法，我们只需要看看未来几十年会如何发展。

Videos aren’t books, and ereaders aren’t TVs.

The book/written word is also a technology that has preferences on how it’s used and transformations that it imposes on its users, and they’re much different than those of TV or computers.

书籍/书面文字也是一种技术，它在使用方式和施加给用户的转换方面有偏好，它们与电视或计算机有很大不同。

comments.author: agentultra
comments.textRawHtml:
“The knowledge is not in danger, in fact, it has never been safer. The AI models have read every manual that no human reads.”

I disagree. If you ask a model for a manual and it regurgitates that manual from its training data, it’s over-fitted. It will regurgitate something that looks like a training manual. Or whatever fits your query about training manuals.

You still have to push back on them sometimes when you spot an error. And you can only spot them if you already know what you’re looking for and should expect. Otherwise you have to ignore the output and just get the links which… could be outdated or made up as well. You’ll never know until you verify the results.

And this degrades with compression and time.

There’s no royal road. I agree that trying and getting frustrated and having to take the effort to understand something pays off in spades. I just think it’s still worth it and vastly under appreciated in this era of “everything fast, now.”
comments.textZhHtml:
“知识并不处于危险之中，事实上，它从未如此安全。人工智能模型已经阅读了人类不会阅读的所有手册。”

我不同意。如果你向模型索要一本手册，而它却从训练数据中反省出该手册，那么它就过度拟合了。它会反刍一些看起来像培训手册的东西。或者任何适合您有关培训手册的查询。

有时当你发现错误时，你仍然需要阻止他们。只有当您已经知道自己在寻找什么并且应该期待什么时，您才能发现它们。否则，您必须忽略输出并仅获取可能已过时或已编造的链接。在验证结果之前，您永远不会知道。

这会随着压缩和时间的推移而降低。

没有皇家大道。我同意尝试并感到沮丧并必须付出努力去理解某些事情肯定会得到回报。我只是认为它仍然是值得的，并且在这个“一切都快，现在”的时代被大大低估了。

children.author: StanislavPetrov
children.textRawHtml:
>"Young people know all about computers" was never true either.

That's true, but only because not all young people owned or used computers. I was born in the mid 70s and got my first computer as a young child. Back then you had to "know all about computers" to use the computer and get it to function. These days, every kid grows up surrounded by computers, with a computer in their pocket, and very few have even an inkling of how they work.

Thus "young people who use computers know all about computers" used to be a true statement, but no longer is.
children.textZhHtml:
>“年轻人对计算机了如指掌”也从来都不是真的。

确实如此，但只是因为并非所有年轻人都拥有或使用计算机。 我出生于 70 年代中期，小时候就拥有了第一台电脑。 那时，您必须“了解有关计算机的所有知识”才能使用计算机并使其正常运行。如今，每个孩子都是在电脑的包围下长大的，口袋里装着一台电脑，但很少有人知道它们是如何工作的。

因此，“使用计算机的年轻人都了解计算机”曾经是一个真实的说法，但现在不再如此。

children.author: WJW
children.textRawHtml:
It wouldn't have been true even if all young people in our generation would have had all the access to computers they would've liked. Most people just don't like tinkering with technology and that's fine!

The whole argument is pretty dumb anyway. Just try replacing "computers" with any other technology and you see how weird it starts sounding.

- "People use roads every day but almost nobody even knows how to make asphalt from scratch these days."

- "People use electricity every day but almost nobody even knows the basic equations for electric generators."

- "Children these days use refrigerators only as consumers, they don't even understand the differences between HCFC and A3 type refrigerants..."

Computer programmers despairing about how few children understand computers has strong vibes from https://xkcd.com/2501/ IMO.

即使我们这一代的所有年轻人都能使用他们想要的计算机，情况也不会如此。大多数人就是不喜欢摆弄技术 没关系！

无论如何，整个论点都很愚蠢。只要尝试用任何其他技术替换“计算机”，您就会发现它听起来有多么奇怪。

- “人们每天都在使用道路，但现在几乎没有人知道如何从头开始制造沥青。”

- “人们每天都用电，但几乎没有人知道发电机的基本方程。”

- “现在的孩子只是作为消费者使用冰箱，他们甚至不了解 HCFC 和 A3 类制冷剂之间的区别......”

计算机程序员对了解计算机的孩子如此之少感到绝望，这引起了强烈的共鸣 https://xkcd.com/2501/ 国际海事组织。

children.author: Barrin92
children.textRawHtml:
maybe another angle to this that I observed: my working class dad who had little education happily taught himself a bit of programming with computer magazines in the 80s an 90s. He actually could figure out his way around a command prompt.

I went on to study CS and teach undergrad courses and what I noticed, this started already maybe a decade ago, is that CS students who we handed bootable linux usbs couldn't figure out how to set their system up. And not just that, they just kept emailing us with statements like "it didn't work, what do I do?". It's not just lack of knowledge but complete helplessness when something doesn't work in 2 minutes. That's the biggest problem with this reliance on ChatGPT or whatever.

I think the young generation is in an even worse position. Not only do they not know how computers work, they don't even have the basic DIY problem solving attitude our parents have.
children.textZhHtml:
也许我观察到的另一个角度是：我的工人阶级父亲几乎没有受过什么教育，他很高兴在 80 年代和 90 年代通过计算机杂志自学了一些编程。他实际上可以通过命令提示找到自己的方法。

我继续学习 CS 并教授本科课程，我注意到，这可能已经在十年前开始了，我们向 CS 学生提供了可启动的 Linux USB，但他们不知道如何设置他们的系统。不仅如此，他们还不断给我们发电子邮件，附上诸如“这不起作用，我该怎么办？”之类的声明。当某件事在 2 分钟内不起作用时，这不仅仅是缺乏知识，而是完全无助。这是依赖 ChatGPT 或其他什么的最大问题。

我认为年轻一代的处境更糟。他们不仅不知道计算机是如何工作的，甚至没有我们父母那样基本的DIY解决问题的态度。

comments.author: woodruffw
comments.textRawHtml:
> You opened files like autoexec.bat and you read them. Sometimes you built a boot disk for a single game. A floppy whose entire reason to exist was to start the computer in the configuration that one program demanded.

Is the premise here correct? I'm not sure that I'm convinced that a 1990s computer user who knew how to edit autoexec.bat or insert a floppy to boot their computer "knew how it worked" in a meaningful sense.

The stack of abstractions is deeper now, and all indications suggest it's not going to stop deepening. But I think the abstractions were already quite deep by the 1990s.

(I think the classic error here is a demographic one: computer nerds always poke through abstractions, because they do it for fun. I don't think that's going to stop, anymore than web browsers stopped people from writing kernels. If anything, we write more low-level code than ever, because access to the prerequisite knowledge is less gatekept than before.)
comments.textZhHtml:
> 您打开了 autoexec.bat 之类的文件并阅读了它们。有时您会为单个游戏制作启动盘。一张软盘，其存在的全部原因是按照一个程序所需的配置启动计算机。

这里的前提正确吗？我不确定我是否相信 20 世纪 90 年代的计算机用户知道如何编辑 autoexec.bat 或插入软盘来启动计算机，从而在有意义的意义上“知道它是如何工作的”。

现在抽象的堆栈更深了，所有迹象都表明它不会 停止 深化。但我认为到 20 世纪 90 年代，抽象已经相当深入了。

（我认为这里的经典错误是人口统计错误：计算机迷 总是 探究抽象事物，因为他们这样做是为了好玩。我认为这种情况不会停止，就像网络浏览器阻止人们编写内核一样。如果有的话，我们写 更多的 低级代码比以往任何时候都少，因为对先决知识的访问比以前更少受到限制。）

>The modem sang its negotiation out loud, and that shriek was two machines arguing about how to speak to each other, and after enough times you could hear when the argument was going south and the call was about to drop. children.author: klibertp
children.textRawHtml:
That other post is just plain wrong. LLMs are just about the only thing that can meaningfully benefit from having access to almost all the code your environment is built from. If you're on Linux, the agents not only have all that code accessible in web search, but also almost certainly include most of it in their training data.

If anything, agents are an opportunity for the "Linux year on the desktop". Granted, I don't personally believe Linux needs to have that year; it's just fine without any mass adoption there. It's simply no longer that important a segment. I thought most of the debate around that also died out years ago (perhaps even a decade?)

Still, I recently had the opportunity to compare how Codex configures macOS and Fedora for the same task, and the latter was a bit faster and significantly cheaper (in tokens). That's obviously anecdata - maybe the particular task was overrepresented on one side or something - but I wouldn't be surprised if this was a more general trend.
children.textZhHtml:
另一篇文章完全是错误的。 LLM 几乎是唯一可以从访问构建您的环境的几乎所有代码中获益的东西。如果您使用的是 Linux，代理不仅可以通过网络搜索访问所有代码，而且几乎可以肯定将其中大部分代码包含在其训练数据中。

如果说有什么不同的话，那就是代理是“桌面 Linux 年”的一个机会。当然，我个人并不相信 Linux 需要 拥有那一年；没有大规模采用就很好了。它根本不再是那么重要的一个部分。我认为围绕这​​个问题的大部分争论几年前就消失了（也许甚至是十年？）

不过，我最近有机会比较 Codex 如何配置 macOS 和 Fedora 来执行相同的任务，后者速度更快，而且成本明显更低（以代币计算）。这显然是轶事——也许特定任务在一方面或其他方面被过度代表——但如果这是一个更普遍的趋势，我不会感到惊讶。

comments.author: mikewarot
comments.textRawHtml:
>The knowledge is not in danger, in fact, it has never been safer. The AI models have read every manual that no human reads. They will recite, flawlessly and forever, exactly how all machines work. If this were only about competence, it would be the most secure moment in the history of computing.

Obviously this person doesn't have enough experience with the current crop of AIs. This is the exact reason why we should be concerned in the first place. Someone has to design and build the next version of everything, and AI isn't qualified to do the job, and might never be.

>What is dying is acquaintance. The plain, unglamorous intimacy of having fought a particular machine, and lost, and gone back, and finally felt the thing give.

This is true. But then again, who among us had to get up in the middle of the night, and trudge out to an outhouse? Who had to shovel a bit more coal into the furnace? Who had to pump water, and heat it on the stove? Who had to split wood, and stack it for the winter?

There are many, many things we're no longer acquainted with, and that's sad, but still ok. What's not ok is losing the competence required to maintaining the infrastructure and supply chain supporting society and civilization.

有很多很多的事情我们不再熟悉，这很悲伤，但也没关系。什么是 不好 正在失去维持支持社会和文明的基础设施和供应链所需的能力。

comments.author: kev009
comments.textRawHtml:
I came into the US tech industry in 2011 from a CS program (programming on my own longer), which was basically a pipeline into Java or web dev at that time (the classes on other things like OS, compilers, and FPGA were a thing, but seen by most as a hurdle not a promising or practical career pathways).

Most of the people I dealt with for the decade following that (i.e. pre-LLM) had little clue how any part of computers worked, and I don't intend that to be particularly pejorative, it's just that it's been somewhat easy to carve out a niche doing something to do with programming while having little understanding of the small or big picture.. all heuristics and applying working patterns.. for a few decades.

You can still understand whatever you want, today, with added tools. It's just a choice to turn your brain on or off. I think LLMs are perfectly fine as a learning tool to interrogate a subject, do comparatives, and then formalize your understanding by reading the sources. At a macro level brain rot is real, but it cuts across all generations and it started long before LLMs.
comments.textZhHtml:
我于 2011 年从 CS 项目（我自己编程的时间更长）进入美国科技行业，这基本上是当时进入 Java 或 Web 开发的管道（有关操作系统、编译器和 FPGA 等其他内容的课程是一件事，但大多数人认为这是一个障碍，而不是有前途或实用的职业道路）。

在接下来的十年里，我接触过的大多数人（即法学硕士预科生）对计算机的任何部分是如何工作的几乎一无所知，我并不是想特别贬义，只是在对小或大的情况知之甚少的情况下，很容易开拓出一个与编程相关的利基市场..所有的启发式方法和应用工作模式..几十年来。

今天，通过添加工具，您仍然可以理解您想要的任何内容。 这只是打开或关闭大脑的选择。 我认为法学硕士非常适合作为一种学习工具来探究一个主题，进行比较，然后通过阅读源代码来形式化你的理解。 从宏观层面来看，脑腐病是真实存在的，但它跨越了所有世代，而且早在法学硕士之前就开始了。

comments.author: overgard
comments.textRawHtml:
This might just be a personality trait of me, but I really like technology that's transparent enough that I can fix/modify/understand it. Opaque technology gives me an ick. It's not really a complexity thing -- Linux has this property of being transparent even though it's extremely complex. The way he describes old computers.. man, I loved that about them, there was nothing _secret_ going on, you weren't worried about telemetry or weird background processes. Then again, I still drive a manual transmission internal combustion car in 2026, so maybe I don't represent the world.

Then again, maybe there's light at the end of the tunnel. Even though AI boosters kind of annoy me, I do appreciate that they're tinkering and hacking and working to understand the technology on some level. I think there's always going to be some small number of people that are like this, in the same way there are still people that look at disassembly and understand CPU caches.
comments.textZhHtml:
这可能只是我的性格特征，但我真的很喜欢足够透明的技术，我可以修复/修改/理解它。不透明的技术让我感到恶心。这并不是真正的复杂性——Linux 具有透明的特性，尽管它极其复杂。他描述旧计算机的方式......伙计，我喜欢它们，没有什么秘密发生，你不担心遥测或奇怪的后台进程。话又说回来，2026年我还是开手动挡内燃车，所以也许我不能代表世界。

话又说回来，也许隧道尽头就有光明。尽管人工智能助推器有点让我恼火，但我确实很欣赏他们的修补和黑客行为，并努力在某种程度上理解这项技术。我认为总会有一小部分人是这样的，同样，仍然有人关注反汇编并理解 CPU 缓存。

As someone who played plenty of computer games as a teen in the 90s I assure you that I did not know how it worked. I learned how it worked later when I got a CS degree at university. comments.author: nostrademons
comments.textRawHtml:
This is sort of the story of the telephone system of the 1950s-1970s, or electricity in the early 1900s, or cars from 1950-1980s, or airplanes from 1910-1939.

I have no idea how an electrical transformer works (well, other than the bare theory I learned in physics courses), or how power gets from the power company to my house, or how the circuits in my home are setup. I plug something in, and it works, and occasionally I throw a breaker if something is malfunctioning. There's no resistance there (pun not intended), and there shouldn't be. People got killed trying to wire their own homes.

I used to read about phone phreaks from the 1970s that could do black magic to get free long-distance phone calls. When I grew up in the 80s, that was basically gone. You picked up the phone, got a dial-tone, and called. And now it's really gone, with everyone having an encrypted cell phone connection over 5G, and your IMEI and IMSI being phoned home to every tower you connect to.

It's the nature of technology and capitalism. As the technology matures, it gets hidden away to become increasingly invisible to the end user, so you just do what you want to do with it. And then the engineering resources get spent on new problems.
comments.textZhHtml:
这有点像 20 世纪 50 年代至 1970 年代的电话系统、1900 年代初的电力、1950 至 1980 年代的汽车、或 1910 至 1939 年代的飞机的故事。

我不知道变压器是如何工作的（好吧，除了我在物理课程中学到的基本理论之外），也不知道电力如何从电力公司到我的房子，或者我家里的电路是如何设置的。 我插入一些东西，它就可以工作，如果有东西出现故障，我偶尔会扔掉一个断路器。 那里没有阻力（不是双关语），也不应该有。 人们在试图给自己的家布线时被杀。

我曾经读到过 20 世纪 70 年代的电话飞客可以使用黑魔法来获得免费的长途电话。 当我在80年代长大时，这种情况基本上消失了。 您拿起电话，听到拨号音，然后拨打了电话。 现在是 真的 每个人都可以通过 5G 进行加密的手机连接，并且您的 IMEI 和 IMSI 会被传回您连接的每个塔。

这是技术和资本主义的本质。 随着技术的成熟，它会被隐藏起来，对最终用户来说越来越不可见，所以你只需用它做你想做的事。 然后工程资源就会花在新的问题上。

comments.author: pseudosavant
comments.textRawHtml:
I can't help but think this type of step function change has happened with computers before.

The change from punch cards to magnetic storage certainly made it so you didn't have to "know how it works" for every single bit.

The change from machine code to a language like Fortran brought about such an abstraction that a Fortran dev didn't "know how it works" at that same level anymore.

At this point, the layers of abstractions between using a React component and something being rendered is immense. React VDOM, the real DOM, browser render engine (which sits on abstractions like ANGLE, skia, etc), calls OS APIs, which call driver APIs, and the lowest level of anything it is still C/C++ that is compiled (abstracted) to something closer to what the hardware expects.

I won't bore you with the ChatGPT output details, but it estimated at least 35 meaningful layers of abstraction between a React component and being rendered to the screen. LLMs seem to be the latest level of abstraction to make it so we "know how it works" less than before.
comments.textZhHtml:
我不禁想到这种类型的阶跃函数变化以前在计算机上也发生过。

从打孔卡到磁存储的变化无疑使您不必“知道它是如何工作的”每一位。

从机器代码到 Fortran 这样的语言的变化带来了这样的抽象，以至于 Fortran 开发人员不再在同一级别“知道它是如何工作的”。

此时，使用 React 组件和渲染内容之间的抽象层是巨大的。 React VDOM，真正的 DOM，浏览器渲染引擎（位于像 ANGLE、skia 等这样的抽象之上），调用操作系统 API，操作系统 API 调用驱动程序 API，而最低级别的东西仍然是 C/C++，被编译（抽象）为更接近硬件期望的东西。

我不会让您厌烦 ChatGPT 输出的详细信息，但它估计 React 组件和渲染到屏幕之间至少有 35 个有意义的抽象层。法学硕士似乎是最新的抽象水平，因此我们比以前更“了解它是如何运作的”。

children.author: legobmw99
children.textRawHtml:
It's hard to articulate why, but 35 layers of deterministic abstraction feels very different from 1 layer of nondeterministic one.

In particular, I think every developer has experienced the need to jump "down the stack" to debug or understand something (even if not all the way down). Certainly, I think any senior developer should be at least conversant in the first few levels below wherever they "live". But this seemingly ends up looking fundamentally different in the interaction mode of an LLM, because you'd just ask it to jump down the stack for you
children.textZhHtml:
很难说清原因，但 35 层确定性抽象与 1 层非确定性抽象感觉非常不同。

特别是，我认为每个开发人员都经历过需要“跳到堆栈”来调试或理解某些内容（即使不是一直跳下去）。当然，我认为任何高级开发人员都应该至少熟悉他们“居住”的地方的前几个级别。但这看起来在法学硕士的交互模式中看起来根本不同，因为你只是问 它 为您跳下堆栈

children.author: pseudosavant
children.textRawHtml:
I still see this more like there will be "devs" that only know how to use LLMs and not any layer under it.

Just like there were web devs that only knew jQuery but not any actual JS. Game devs that know only Unreal Engine or Unity but nothing about DirectX, OpenGL, or Vulkan. C/C++ devs that know nothing about LLVM IR or the actual bits their compiler generates. It can also be a poor understanding of the layers in the abstractions above you too.

I find a lot of value in being able to understand levels beneath the layer I'm mostly working in. There are things, even/especially with LLMs, that I can grasp because of my deeper understanding.

But there have been plenty in our field that have only known their level of the abstraction for quite a long time. Every time there is something introduced that lowers the barrier to entry to making things, there is a version of this "but they don't know how it really works..."
children.textZhHtml:
我仍然认为这更像是会有“开发人员”只知道如何使用法学硕士而不知道其下的任何层。

就像有些 Web 开发人员只知道 jQuery 但不知道任何实际的 JS 一样。只了解 Unreal Engine 或 Unity，但对 DirectX、OpenGL 或 Vulkan 一无所知的游戏开发人员。 C/C++ 开发人员对 LLVM IR 或其编译器生成的实际位一无所知。这也可能是对你上面的抽象层的理解很差。

我发现能够理解我主要工作的层次之下的层次有很多价值。有些事情，甚至/特别是法学硕士，我可以因为我更深入的理解而掌握。

但在我们的领域中，有很多人在很长一段时间内才知道他们的抽象级别。每当有一些东西被引入来降低制造事物的准入门槛时，就会有一个版本的“但他们不知道它到底是如何运作的......”

children.author: tacostakohashi
children.textRawHtml:
I feel that things are pretty different. Although the example of interrupts and jumper settings being common knowledge is a bit of a stretch... it's still amazing to recall that MS-DOS was regarded as an end-user / consumer OS, and that, more generally, it really was regarded as totally normal to need to invest some time in learning about the system, files, directories, typing, configuring/customizing settings and network options just to be able to do what you wanted to do.

I find the current expectations around consumer "apps" to be totally infantile in comparison, where everything is now a single-purpose "app" that does exactly one thing when you push a button, and if you want something even a tiny bit different.. you can't, and that even basic things like files and settings are no longer accessible.
children.textZhHtml:
我觉得事情已经很不一样了。虽然中断和跳线设置是常识的例子有点牵强……但回想起 MS-DOS 被视为最终用户/消费者操作系统，而且更一般地说，需要投入一些时间来学习系统、文件、目录、打字、配置/自定义设置和网络选项，只是为了能够做你想做的事情，这确实被认为是完全正常的。

相比之下，我发现目前对消费者“应用程序”的期望完全是幼稚的，现在一切都是一个单一用途的“应用程序”，当你按下按钮时，它只做一件事，如果你想要一些东西，哪怕是一点点不同……你也不能，甚至连文件和设置等基本的东西都不再可访问。

comments.author: VorpalWay

comments.author: baconmania
comments.textRawHtml: Folks who keep mentioning that this is no different than any previous upward leap of abstraction in human history are missing a key point that the frontier labs are certainly not missing: this is the first time in computing that you are becoming completely dependent on a _subscription service_. I don't need to know how my CPU works because it continues to work once built. Once I outsource all cognition to a billable service, I am forever and continuously in thrall to someone else's revenue strategy.

children.author: bitwize
children.textRawHtml:
Windows 3.x did not cohabitate well with certain games that required nearly the entire 640k of conventional memory to run. These are mainly games from the old DOS days; games that relied on DPMI were largely exempt. But to play certain Sierra titles, for instance, you had to set up one CONFIG.SYS configuration that loaded HIMEM.SYS and EMM386.EXE for Windows and another that left them out to free up that memory for your games. In later versions of DOS you could set up a menu to select the appropriate configuration at boot.

And of course IRQ diddling was still necessary to configure sound, network, and game controller hardware throughout the DOS era of gaming, which lasted well into the Windows 9x era.
children.textZhHtml:
Windows 3.x 与某些需要几乎全部 640k 传统内存才能运行的游戏不能很好地共存。这些主要是旧 DOS 时代的游戏；依赖 DPMI 的游戏基本上不受此影响。但例如，要玩某些 Sierra 游戏，您必须设置一个 CONFIG.SYS 配置来加载 Windows 的 HIMEM.SYS 和 EMM386.EXE，而另一种配置则将它们排除在外，以便为您的游戏释放内存。在更高版本的 DOS 中，您可以设置一个菜单来在启动时选择适当的配置。

当然，在整个 DOS 游戏时代，IRQ 欺骗仍然是配置声音、网络和游戏控制器硬件所必需的，这种情况一直持续到 Windows 9x 时代。

comments.author: deweywsu
comments.textRawHtml:
I may not be a "typical" engineer as much anymore, in that I don't seem to be excited by knowing the intricate details of how things work as much as I enjoy knowing the capabilities of the systems that enable the projects I work on, and working at a higher level that might be more abstract and conceptual.  AI enables that very well in that it enables jumping between paradigms and enabling creativity by removing a lot of the grind that was necessary in gathering technical details.  I find it less appealing now to get into the weeds of how a thing works, even though at 10 I very much did.  Like the author, I dug into the details of exactly which bits needed to be activated to make a particular program work.  But as I aged, I was drawn to higher levels of abstraction, I think because I realized I could do more overall by piecing together bigger blocks.  Yet I know colleagues who like to be involved in a very granular level of detail.  I have many friends who almost revel in their knowledge that they are the ones who know precisely why something works the way it does.  I think about the story of the NASA engineer on the Apollo 11 program who understood the assembly so well that he could confidently answer on the spot the moment the radar overload "1201 alarm" error flashed that the mission was still good to go.  Some folks get so deep in this perspective that it almost seems like an affront to suggest they may be wrong about one of those details.  And well they should, because precise people are very much needed to understand intricate systems.
But it makes me wonder how they feel with AI being an indeterminate system - one where you can never really know what the answer will be every time.  Likely still correct, and getting better every day, but also possibly different every time.  Added to that, it's exponential spread across the software engineering domain.  For me, even as someone who likes high-level systems, I still relate to the feeling that the detailed part of me that still likes some of the internals may be less focused on in the future.  Even for me that's kind of sad.  But I am also curious about my colleagues who are super detail oriented - how do you feel about AI and how it changes the focus on that detail aspect?
comments.textZhHtml:
我可能不再是一个“典型的”工程师了，因为我似乎不再对了解事物如何工作的复杂细节感到兴奋，而是喜欢了解支持我所从事项目的系统的功能，并在可能更抽象和概念化的更高级别上工作。  人工智能很好地实现了这一点，因为它可以在范式之间跳跃，并通过消除收集技术细节所需的大量苦差事来实现创造力。  我发现现在深入了解事物的运作方式不再那么有吸引力了，尽管我在 10 岁时就已经深入了解了。  像作者一样，我深入研究了需要激活哪些位才能使特定程序运行的细节。  但随着年龄的增长，我被更高层次的抽象所吸引，我想是因为我意识到我可以通过拼凑更大的块来完成更多的整体工作。  然而，我认识一些喜欢参与非常细致的细节的同事。  我有很多朋友，他们几乎陶醉于自己的知识，因为他们知道为什么某些事情会这样运作。  我想起阿波罗 11 号计划中的 NASA 工程师的故事，他对组装非常了解，以至于当雷达过载“1201 警报”错误闪现时，他可以在现场自信地回答任务仍然可以继续进行。  有些人对这一观点的理解如此之深，以至于暗示他们可能对其中一个细节可能是错误的，这似乎是一种侮辱。  他们应该这样做，因为非常需要精确的人来理解复杂的系统。
但这让我想知道他们对人工智能作为一个不确定的系统有何感受——你永远无法真正知道每次的答案是什么。  可能仍然是正确的，并且每天都在进步，但也可能每次都不同。  除此之外，它在软件工程领域呈指数级传播。  对我来说，即使作为一个喜欢高级系统的人，我仍然有这样的感觉：我仍然喜欢一些内部的细节部分，将来可能会不太关注。  即使对我来说这也是一种悲伤。  但我也对那些超级注重细节的同事感到好奇——你对人工智能有何看法，以及它如何改变对细节方面的关注？
