children.author: klibertp
children.textRawHtml:
> How pessimistic you are on this subject depends on how much you believe in domain transfer for these tools.

I tried to force various LLMs to generate many strange languages for the past 3 years. From Raku to Smalltalk, to Common Lisp and Janet, to Prolog, D, Nim, Zig, Elixir, F#, etc.

It's a mess.

They do a mostly good job with language semantics. Agents can also use standard tooling to catch and fix syntax errors. So far so good, though if a given language provides an unusual implementation for a popular feature, the model can get confused. It's usually easy to spot.

The problem is with the ecosystems. They tend to be tiny, and for new languages, they will be tiny almost by definition. As a result, the vast majority of the generated code either reimplements things from scratch or hallucinates dependencies. Since the agents iterate and the hallucinated deps don't materialize, the model fixes that... more often than not by writing from scratch. Or leaving a `// TODO:` comment.

What I found helpful is straight-up vendoring deps as submodules. Armed with grep and sources, they are much more likely to produce working code. But that also means you still need to handle the dependencies yourself, while it'll "just work" for a Kotlin/Gradle project.

It doesn't matter for all projects to the same degree, of course, but it is a pain point that will add to the difficulty (sky-high already) of a new language getting any traction, unfortunately :(
children.textZhHtml:
> 您对此主题的悲观程度取决于您对这些工具的域名转移的信任程度。

在过去的三年里，我试图强迫各个法学硕士生成许多奇怪的语言。从 Raku 到 Smalltalk，到 Common Lisp 和 Janet，到 Prolog、D、Nim、Zig、Elixir、F# 等。

真是一团糟。

他们在语言语义方面做得很好。代理还可以使用标准工具来捕获和修复语法错误。到目前为止一切顺利，但如果给定语言为流行功能提供了不寻常的实现，则模型可能会感到困惑。通常很容易发现。

问题出在生态系统上。它们往往很小，对于新语言来说，它们几乎按照定义来说很小。结果，绝大多数生成的代码要么从头开始重新实现，要么产生依赖关系。由于代理会迭代并且幻觉的部门不会实现，因此模型通常会通过从头开始编写来解决这个问题。或者留下 `// TODO:` 评论。

我发现有用的是直接将供应商部门作为子模块。有了 grep 和源代码，他们更有可能生成工作代码。但这也意味着您仍然需要自己处理依赖项，而它对于 Kotlin/Gradle 项目“只是有效”。

当然，对于所有项目来说，这并不重要，但不幸的是，这是一个痛点，会增加新语言获得牵引力的难度（已经很高了）:(

comments.author: bigstrat2003
comments.textRawHtml:
> The knowledge is not in danger, in fact, it has never been safer. The AI models have read every manual that no human reads. They will recite, flawlessly and forever, exactly how all machines work.

That's wrong, and that's exactly why the loss of knowledge is such a problem. LLMs do not, and cannot, actually know a single thing. They are a statistical model, not knowledge. When they give out wrong information (and they always will, by their very nature), you need someone with actual knowledge to be able to recognize the BS and correct it. But we are losing the knowledge, and unless things change we will be no better off than the people in dystopian sci-fi stories who pray to the machine god because nobody knows how it actually works.
comments.textZhHtml:

这是错误的，这正是知识丢失成为一个问题的原因。 LLM 不会，并且 不能，实际上只知道一件事。它们是统计模型，而不是知识。当他们给出错误信息时（他们总是会，就其本质而言），您需要具有实际知识的人才能识别BS并纠正它。但我们正在失去知识，除非事情发生改变，否则我们不会比反乌托邦科幻故事中向机器神祈祷的人更好，因为没有人知道它实际上是如何运作的。

children.textRawHtml: Yes. And since LLMs can not improve knowledge - I mean, they can generate new arrangements of information, but they have no idea whether any of it real or making sense, unless humans - explicitly or through training - tell them, the more we rely on LLM knowledge the less the quality of it would be. Right now the LLMs are mainly in auxiliary role, so most of the knowledge erosion they generate is laughed at and relatively quickly corrected. But would this hold once the role of generative AIs increases? We already essentially entered the chaos period with news content - there's so much noise that it's basically impossible to know if any news message you read is true or manipulated somehow. This is going to start happening to more fundamental knowledge too, either on purpose or just by the force of the probabilistic nature of generative AI.
children.textZhHtml: 是的。由于法学硕士无法提高知识——我的意思是，他们可以生成新的信息安排，但他们不知道这些信息是否真实或有意义，除非人类——明确地或通过培训——告诉他们，我们越依赖法学硕士知识，其质量就越低。目前LLM主要起辅助作用，因此他们产生的知识侵蚀大多会被嘲笑并相对较快地得到纠正。但一旦生成式人工智能的作用增强，这种情况还会成立吗？我们基本上已经进入了新闻内容的混乱时期——噪音如此之多，以至于基本上不可能知道你读到的任何新闻消息是真实的还是以某种方式被操纵的。这也将开始发生在更基础的知识上，无论是有意的还是仅仅由于生成人工智能的概率性质的力量。

comments.author: sublinear
comments.textRawHtml:
Does anyone have a list of all the times this sentiment has been incorrect over the decades?

There's no arguing down that we have a greater amount of information about old hardware and software easily available now compared to back then, and there are many more enthusiasts as well.

This kind of cognitive dissonance has been going on in other disciplines, like music, since their inception. Guess what? There's thousands of highly talented young people on social media covering it all.

What people are actually upset about is that there isn't new "old stuff" and they can't make money off what can now be thoroughly understood by anyone.

Don't get me wrong, AI is a total shitshow and we're entering another winter, but there's also no reason to be jealous of what is making money right now.

comments.author: CPLX
comments.textRawHtml:
Who cares?

There have always been layers of abstraction. I've been around for a while, and when I was a kid, the two choices I remember seeing were assembly code and simple semantic languages like BASIC.

Assembly seemed like too cryptic for me to really even follow and I never really did learn it, but at the time I remember people would say that assembly was easy and basically plain English compared to machine code.

As recently as fifteen or twenty years ago, I would occasionally check in and think of how unbelievably far away we had gotten from how the computer actually works. Like, you can just write "open window" and a window opens. Amazing.

Of course, those people writing machine code didn't need to really understand what P and N were in a transistor, let alone how an integrated circuit pulls it all together. And I'm not sure how much those guys knew about silicon dioxide.

The more complex things get and the more layers of abstraction there are, the more impossible it gets to really master things all the way down to first principles.

So what? People can carve out whatever chunk of the stack they want to really understand if they want to focus their lives on it. And for everyone else who's just trying to accomplish some other goal with computers as the tool, they will naturally use the highest level of abstraction and the simplest one for them to use, which is exactly what they should do.
comments.textZhHtml:
谁在乎？

总是存在抽象层。我已经有一段时间了，当我还是个孩子的时候，我记得看到的两种选择是汇编代码和像 BASIC 这样的简单语义语言。

汇编对我来说似乎太神秘了，甚至无法真正理解，而且我从未真正学习过它，但当时我记得人们会说，与机器代码相比，汇编很简单，基本上是简单的英语。

就在十五或二十年前，我偶尔会回顾一下，想想我们与计算机的实际工作方式相差了多么令人难以置信的遥远。就像，你可以只写“打开窗口”，然后就会打开一个窗口。惊人的。

当然，那些编写机器代码的人不需要真正了解晶体管中的 P 和 N 是什么，更不用说集成电路如何将它们组合在一起了。我不确定那些人对二氧化硅了解多少。

事情变得越复杂，抽象层越多，就越不可能真正掌握事物的第一原则。

所以呢？如果人们想把自己的生活集中在它上面，就可以将他们想要真正理解的堆栈中的任何部分切出来。对于其他只是想用计算机作为工具来完成其他目标的人来说，他们自然会使用最高级别的抽象和最简单的抽象，这正是他们应该做的。

显然，纯人工智能写作有所增加，但我发现这类“分析”内容的服务弊大于利。

children.author: blks
children.textRawHtml: “Pure AI” so you haven’t used any LLM at all, or just partially?
children.textZhHtml: “纯人工智能”，所以你根本没有使用过任何法学硕士，或者只是部分使用过？

children.author: b40d-48b2-979e
children.textRawHtml: No one questioned that you're human, but the writing is very apparently AI-written.
children.textZhHtml: 没有人质疑你是人类，但这篇文章显然是人工智能写的。

children.author: ryandrake
children.textRawHtml: Pretty much every HN article now has someone in the comment section chiming in to complain "This is AI".
children.textZhHtml: 现在，几乎每篇 HN 文章的评论部分都会有人插话抱怨“这就是人工智能”。

children.author: roywiggins
children.textRawHtml:
Sometimes that's me. I wish more submissions didn't read like AI. I worry that people can't detect tiresomely LLMy output and upvote it to the front page. I'm not mad or anything, I just find the internet less and less useful, because so much that circulates pings my personal AI detector (running in my skull). Sometimes I just close the tab in frustration but sometimes I comment, because it sucks.

(I don't actually care that much if it is LLM text, I care when something reads like it is, because it means everyone writing blogs using this style sounds like everyone else. it's alienating!)
children.textZhHtml:
有时那就是我。我希望更多的提交内容读起来不像人工智能。我担心人们无法检测到令人厌烦的 LLMy 输出并将其投票到首页。我并没有生气或者什么，我只是发现互联网越来越没用了，因为太多的传播会影响我的个人人工智能探测器（在我的头骨中运行）。有时我只是沮丧地关闭选项卡，但有时我会发表评论，因为它很糟糕。

（如果它是法学硕士文本，我实际上并不太关心，我关心的是当某些内容读起来像它的样子时，因为这意味着每个使用这种风格编写博客的人听起来都像其他人一样。这是疏远的！）

children.author: no_multitudes
children.textRawHtml: It is interesting that your recent posts have much more LLM style tics than your post on the same site from 2023. Are you deliberately imitating LLM writing quirks?
children.textZhHtml: 有趣的是，你最近的帖子比 2023 年在同一网站上发布的帖子有更多的 LLM 风格。你是故意模仿 LLM 写作怪癖吗？

children.author: MajesticHobo2
children.textRawHtml: Why does it have to be deliberate? It's not surprising that people exposed to output from LLMs will unconsciously pick up their linguistic habits.
children.textZhHtml: 为什么一定要刻意呢？接触法学硕士成果的人会无意识地养成他们的语言习惯，这并不奇怪。

children.author: no_multitudes
children.textRawHtml: Maybe it isn't deliberate! I was asking out of curiosity. This reads as extremely LLM-y to me, so if it was purely human-written I am curious about the process.
children.textZhHtml: 也许不是故意的！我是出于好奇才问的。这对我来说非常像法学硕士，所以如果它是纯粹由人类编写的，我对这个过程很好奇。

children.author: roywiggins
children.textRawHtml: Even more worrying if it's not deliberate and we are all just naturally writing like that now.
children.textZhHtml: 更令人担忧的是，如果这不是故意的，而且我们现在都是自然而然地这样写的。

children.author: recursive
children.textRawHtml: I trust these detectors even less than I trust LLMs in the first place.
children.textZhHtml: 我对这些检测器的信任甚至比我对法学硕士的信任还要少。

children.author: nzealand
children.textRawHtml:
The satirically brilliant Genius AI detector correctly indicates it is not AI

https://geniusaidetector.com/
children.textZhHtml:
具有讽刺意味的天才人工智能探测器正确地表明它不是人工智能

https://geniusaidetector.com/

children.author: StanislavPetrov
children.textRawHtml: I submitted a few of my papers from grad school (long before LLMs or so-called "AI" existed) and they were flagged as being ~70% AI.
children.textZhHtml: 我提交了几篇研究生院的论文（早在法学硕士或所谓的“人工智能”存在之前），它们被标记为约 70% 的人工智能。

children.author: tines
children.textRawHtml: I know you know this, but for those reading, these detectors are bullshit.
children.textZhHtml: 我知道你知道这一点，但对于那些阅读的人来说，这些探测器都是废话。

children.author: hackmack10
children.textRawHtml: Damn, that is depressing.  What a shitshow we've created.
children.textZhHtml: 妈的，真是郁闷啊。  我们创造了多么糟糕的节目啊。
